Amsterdam is described in various languages as "پڕ گەلھەترین شار و پایتەختی ھۆڵەند," "Alankomaiden pääkaupunki," "Haaptstad vun Holland," and "Hollanda'nın başkenti ve en büyük şehri," all emphasizing its status as the capital and largest city of the country.

The city covers a total area of 219.32 square kilometers, with 165.76 square kilometers of land and 53.56 square kilometers of water. Amsterdam sits at an elevation of -2.0 meters and includes subdivisions such as Amsterdam-Centrum. The total population is 933,680, while the urban area is home to 1,477,213 people. The city is governed by the municipal council of the Netherlands, uses the area code 020, and has postal codes ranging from 1000 to 1183. Amsterdam observes Central European Time (UTC+1) and Central European Summer Time (UTC+2).

Several notable locations and structures are found in Amsterdam, including Museumplein, Nieuwmarkt, Magere Brug, Python Bridge, Blauwbrug, Enneüs Heerma Bridge, and Nescio Bridge. The city is a key point in major transportation routes such as the Rhine–Alpine Corridor, and serves as a junction or starting point for European routes E19, E22, E35, E231, the A2 motorway, and the IJtunnel.

Amsterdam has played a significant role in history, being the site of events like the Bijltjesoproer, the Anabaptist riot, and the Attack on Amsterdam in 1650. It has served as the capital for the Dutch government-in-exile, the Netherlands-Indonesia Union, the Reichskommissariat Niederlande, and the United Kingdom of the Netherlands, and was the largest city of the Batavian Republic. The city is also home to diverse communities, including Jews, Saint Thomas Christians, Surinamese, Japanese, and Iraqis in the Netherlands.

Many notable individuals are connected to Amsterdam. Maceo Rigters, Rob Witschge, Edo de Waart, and Piet Dickentman were born there, while Emanuel de Witte, Jeremias de Dekker, Matthijs Naiveu, and Piet Dickentman died in the city. Residents have included Jacob Kohnstamm, Niels Lootsma, and Abraham Coster. Amsterdam is also the resting place of Herman Heijermans, Johannes Elison, Titus van Rijn, Harry Mulisch, and Marga Minco. Julius Wolff (mathematician) is noted as a child of Amsterdam.

The city is a hub for organizations and institutions. The International Association for the Evaluation of Educational Achievement, the Union for the International Language Ido, Hollandsche IJzeren Spoorweg-Maatschappij, and Viaplay TV have their headquarters in Amsterdam. The European Grid Infrastructure, Roamler, OLX, WeTransfer, and JetBrains are also located in the city. Tradus was founded in Amsterdam, and Aflatoun and the Kurdistan National Congress were founded by Amsterdam. The Official Museums of Amsterdam and Bank Negara Indonesia serve the region, while the Academic Medical Center and the Amsterdam University of the Arts are based in the city.

Amsterdam is associated with education and training for many individuals, including Frank Scheffer, Stephan Brenninkmeijer, Mochtar Apin, Peter Spier, Hendrik Arent Hamaker, Marc Bauer, Albert Muis, Monika Sosnowska, Benjamin Prins, and Hans Bouman.

Cultural and artistic works are closely tied to Amsterdam. The Night Watch, Tree Roots, and Shoes (Vincent van Gogh) are housed in museums in the city. The play Anne premiered in Amsterdam, and the film Letter to America was produced there. Schilder-boeck was published in Amsterdam, and both Ajax Magazine and De Roode Duivel are based in the city. Bands such as Aux Raus, Loïs Lane, and Area21 call Amsterdam their hometown, and songs like Drowning, Rush Hour, and Not Giving Up on Love were recorded there.

Sports are an important part of Amsterdam’s identity. Jan Wienese, Hans Muller (water polo), Nico Lutkeveld, and Anton Toscani are associated with clubs in the city. ASC De Volewijckers, RKSV DCG, and Jong Ajax play on Amsterdam grounds. Sjaak Swart and Derek Agyakwa have played for Amsterdam teams, and athletes like Calvin Borel, Malakai Black, and Wim Ruska have been billed or raced in the city.

Amsterdam has a history of industry and innovation. The first generation Ford Mustang, the second generation Ford Escort (Europe), and the Mark II Ford Cortina were assembled in Amsterdam. Niek van Dijk has been employed in the city, Til Brugman received an award there, and Maup Caransa is known for his connection to Amsterdam. The cannabis strain Super Lemon Haze originated in the city. Ships such as HNLMS Van Speijk (F802), MV Ondina, and MS Oranje were built in Amsterdam, while Lifeline and Albatros (1899) have Amsterdam as their homeport. The Royal Military Band "Johan Willem Friso" and the Dutch States Navy are garrisoned in the city. Amsterdam is also the owner or operator of ships like Halve Maen, SS J. H. Drummond, and MV Eurabia Sun.

Amsterdam is a destination for airlines such as Helios Airways, Sierra Leone Airways, and Air Atlas Express. The city is also the country for The Social Contract and Leesmuseum voor Vrouwen, and is home to institutions like those of Eduard Willem Walch and Graham Lees. Significant buildings in Amsterdam are associated with Tilman-François Suys and Erick van Egeraat. Theodorus Aaninck represented Amsterdam as a constituency district. Buikslotermeer is a type of Amsterdam area, and the Amstel river has its mouth in the city.

Overall, Amsterdam stands as a vibrant, diverse, and historically significant city, serving as a center for culture, governance, education, industry, and international connections.

Amsterdam is both a municipality and the capital of the Netherlands. Its residents are known as Amsterdammers, and the city’s motto is "Heldhaftig, Vastberaden, Barmhartig" which translates to "Valiant, Steadfast, Compassionate." Amsterdam is described in various languages as "پڕ گەلھەترین شار و پایتەختی ھۆڵەند," "Alankomaiden pääkaupunki," "Haaptstad vun Holland," and "Hollanda'nın başkenti ve en büyük şehri," all emphasizing its status as the capital and largest city of the country.

The city covers a total area of 219.32 square kilometers, with 165.76 square kilometers of land and 53.56 square kilometers of water. Amsterdam sits at an elevation of -2.0 meters and includes subdivisions such as Amsterdam-Centrum. The total population is 933,680, while the urban area is home to 1,477,213 people. The city is governed by the municipal council of the Netherlands, uses the area code 020, and has postal codes ranging from 1000 to 1183. Amsterdam observes Central European Time (UTC+1) and Central European Summer Time (UTC+2).

Several notable locations and structures are found in Amsterdam, including Museumplein, Nieuwmarkt, Magere Brug, Python Bridge, Blauwbrug, Enneüs Heerma Bridge, and Nescio Bridge. The city is a key point in major transportation routes such as the Rhine–Alpine Corridor, and serves as a junction or starting point for European routes E19, E22, E35, E231, the A2 motorway, and the IJtunnel.

Amsterdam has played a significant role in history, being the site of events like the Bijltjesoproer, the Anabaptist riot, and the Attack on Amsterdam in 1650. It has served as the capital for the Dutch government-in-exile, the Netherlands-Indonesia Union, the Reichskommissariat Niederlande, and the United Kingdom of the Netherlands, and was the largest city of the Batavian Republic. The city is also home to diverse communities, including Jews, Saint Thomas Christians, Surinamese, Japanese, and Iraqis in the Netherlands.

Many notable individuals are connected to Amsterdam. Maceo Rigters, Rob Witschge, Edo de Waart, and Piet Dickentman were born there, while Emanuel de Witte, Jeremias de Dekker, Matthijs Naiveu, and Piet Dickentman died in the city. Residents have included Jacob Kohnstamm, Niels Lootsma, and Abraham Coster. Amsterdam is also the resting place of Herman Heijermans, Johannes Elison, Titus van Rijn, Harry Mulisch, and Marga Minco. Julius Wolff (mathematician) is noted as a child of Amsterdam.

The city is a hub for organizations and institutions. The International Association for the Evaluation of Educational Achievement, the Union for the International Language Ido, Hollandsche IJzeren Spoorweg-Maatschappij, and Viaplay TV have their headquarters in Amsterdam. The European Grid Infrastructure, Roamler, OLX, WeTransfer, and JetBrains are also located in the city. Tradus was founded in Amsterdam, and Aflatoun and the Kurdistan National Congress were founded by Amsterdam. The Official Museums of Amsterdam and Bank Negara Indonesia serve the region, while the Academic Medical Center and the Amsterdam University of the Arts are based in the city.

Amsterdam is associated with education and training for many individuals, including Frank Scheffer, Stephan Brenninkmeijer, Mochtar Apin, Peter Spier, Hendrik Arent Hamaker, Marc Bauer, Albert Muis, Monika Sosnowska, Benjamin Prins, and Hans Bouman.

Cultural and artistic works are closely tied to Amsterdam. The Night Watch, Tree Roots, and Shoes (Vincent van Gogh) are housed in museums in the city. The play Anne premiered in Amsterdam, and the film Letter to America was produced there. Schilder-boeck was published in Amsterdam, and both Ajax Magazine and De Roode Duivel are based in the city. Bands such as Aux Raus, Loïs Lane, and Area21 call Amsterdam their hometown, and songs like Drowning, Rush Hour, and Not Giving Up on Love were recorded there.

Sports are an important part of Amsterdam’s identity. Jan Wienese, Hans Muller (water polo), Nico Lutkeveld, and Anton Toscani are associated with clubs in the city. ASC De Volewijckers, RKSV DCG, and Jong Ajax play on Amsterdam grounds. Sjaak Swart and Derek Agyakwa have played for Amsterdam teams, and athletes like Calvin Borel, Malakai Black, and Wim Ruska have been billed or raced in the city.

Amsterdam has a history of industry and innovation. The first generation Ford Mustang, the second generation Ford Escort (Europe), and the Mark II Ford Cortina were assembled in Amsterdam. Niek van Dijk has been employed in the city, Til Brugman received an award there, and Maup Caransa is known for his connection to Amsterdam. The cannabis strain Super Lemon Haze originated in the city. Ships such as HNLMS Van Speijk (F802), MV Ondina, and MS Oranje were built in Amsterdam, while Lifeline and Albatros (1899) have Amsterdam as their homeport. The Royal Military Band "Johan Willem Friso" and the Dutch States Navy are garrisoned in the city. Amsterdam is also the owner or operator of ships like Halve Maen, SS J. H. Drummond, and MV Eurabia Sun.

Amsterdam is a destination for airlines such as Helios Airways, Sierra Leone Airways, and Air Atlas Express. The city is also the country for The Social Contract and Leesmuseum voor Vrouwen, and is home to institutions like those of Eduard Willem Walch and Graham Lees. Significant buildings in Amsterdam are associated with Tilman-François Suys and Erick van Egeraat. Theodorus Aaninck represented Amsterdam as a constituency district. Buikslotermeer is a type of Amsterdam area, and the Amstel river has its mouth in the city.

Overall, Amsterdam stands as a vibrant, diverse, and historically significant city, serving as a center for culture, governance, education, industry, and international connections.

Its residents are known as Amsterdammers, and the city’s motto is "Heldhaftig, Vastberaden, Barmhartig," which translates to "Valiant, Steadfast, Compassionate." Amsterdam is described in various languages as "پڕ گەلھەترین شار و پایتەختی ھۆڵەند," "Alankomaiden pääkaupunki," "Haaptstad vun Holland," and "Hollanda'nın başkenti ve en büyük şehri," all emphasizing its status as the capital and largest city of the country.

The city covers a total area of 219.32 square kilometers, with 165.76 square kilometers of land and 53.56 square kilometers of water. Amsterdam sits at an elevation of -2.0 meters and includes subdivisions such as Amsterdam-Centrum. The total population is 933,680, while the urban area is home to 1,477,213 people. The city is governed by the municipal council of the Netherlands, uses the area code 020, and has postal codes ranging from 1000 to 1183. Amsterdam observes Central European Time (UTC+1) and Central European Summer Time (UTC+2).

Several notable locations and structures are found in Amsterdam, including Museumplein, Nieuwmarkt, Magere Brug, Python Bridge, Blauwbrug, Enneüs Heerma Bridge, and Nescio Bridge. The city is a key point in major transportation routes such as the Rhine–Alpine Corridor, and serves as a junction or starting point for European routes E19, E22, E35, E231, the A2 motorway, and the IJtunnel.

Amsterdam has played a significant role in history, being the site of events like the Bijltjesoproer, the Anabaptist riot, and the Attack on Amsterdam in 1650. It has served as the capital for the Dutch government-in-exile, the Netherlands-Indonesia Union, the Reichskommissariat Niederlande, and the United Kingdom of the Netherlands, and was the largest city of the Batavian Republic. The city is also home to diverse communities, including Jews, Saint Thomas Christians, Surinamese, Japanese, and Iraqis in the Netherlands.

Many notable individuals are connected to Amsterdam. Maceo Rigters, Rob Witschge, Edo de Waart, and Piet Dickentman were born there, while Emanuel de Witte, Jeremias de Dekker, Matthijs Naiveu, and Piet Dickentman died in the city. Residents have included Jacob Kohnstamm, Niels Lootsma, and Abraham Coster. Amsterdam is also the resting place of Herman Heijermans, Johannes Elison, Titus van Rijn, Harry Mulisch, and Marga Minco. Julius Wolff (mathematician) is noted as a child of Amsterdam.

The city is a hub for organizations and institutions. The International Association for the Evaluation of Educational Achievement, the Union for the International Language Ido, Hollandsche IJzeren Spoorweg-Maatschappij, and Viaplay TV have their headquarters in Amsterdam. The European Grid Infrastructure, Roamler, OLX, WeTransfer, and JetBrains are also located in the city. Tradus was founded in Amsterdam, and Aflatoun and the Kurdistan National Congress were founded by Amsterdam. The Official Museums of Amsterdam and Bank Negara Indonesia serve the region, while the Academic Medical Center and the Amsterdam University of the Arts are based in the city.

Amsterdam is associated with education and training for many individuals, including Frank Scheffer, Stephan Brenninkmeijer, Mochtar Apin, Peter Spier, Hendrik Arent Hamaker, Marc Bauer, Albert Muis, Monika Sosnowska, Benjamin Prins, and Hans Bouman.

Cultural and artistic works are closely tied to Amsterdam. The Night Watch, Tree Roots, and Shoes (Vincent van Gogh) are housed in museums in the city. The play Anne premiered in Amsterdam, and the film Letter to America was produced there. Schilder-boeck was published in Amsterdam, and both Ajax Magazine and De Roode Duivel are based in the city. Bands such as Aux Raus, Loïs Lane, and Area21 call Amsterdam their hometown, and songs like Drowning, Rush Hour, and Not Giving Up on Love were recorded there.

Sports are an important part of Amsterdam’s identity. Jan Wienese, Hans Muller (water polo), Nico Lutkeveld, and Anton Toscani are associated with clubs in the city. ASC De Volewijckers, RKSV DCG, and Jong Ajax play on Amsterdam grounds. Sjaak Swart and Derek Agyakwa have played for Amsterdam teams, and athletes like Calvin Borel, Malakai Black, and Wim Ruska have been billed or raced in the city.

Amsterdam has a history of industry and innovation. The first generation Ford Mustang, the second generation Ford Escort (Europe), and the Mark II Ford Cortina were assembled in Amsterdam. Niek van Dijk has been employed in the city, Til Brugman received an award there, and Maup Caransa is known for his connection to Amsterdam. The cannabis strain Super Lemon Haze originated in the city. Ships such as HNLMS Van Speijk (F802), MV Ondina, and MS Oranje were built in Amsterdam, while Lifeline and Albatros (1899) have Amsterdam as their homeport. The Royal Military Band "Johan Willem Friso" and the Dutch States Navy are garrisoned in the city. Amsterdam is also the owner or operator of ships like Halve Maen, SS J. H. Drummond, and MV Eurabia Sun.

Amsterdam is a destination for airlines such as Helios Airways, Sierra Leone Airways, and Air Atlas Express. The city is also the country for The Social Contract and Leesmuseum voor Vrouwen, and is home to institutions like those of Eduard Willem Walch and Graham Lees. Significant buildings in Amsterdam are associated with Tilman-François Suys and Erick van Egeraat. Theodorus Aaninck represented Amsterdam as a constituency district. Buikslotermeer is a type of Amsterdam area, and the Amstel river has its mouth in the city.

Overall, Amsterdam stands as a vibrant, diverse, and historically significant city, serving as a center for culture, governance, education, industry, and international connections.
